title: Evenings In Greece
content:
В В В В In thus connecting together a series of Songs by a thread of poetical narrative, my chief object has been to combine Recitation with Music, so as to enable a greater number of persons to join in the performance, by enlisting as readers those who may not feel willing or competent to take a part as singers.

В В В В The Island of Zea where the scene is laid was called by the ancients Ceos, and was the birthplace of Simonides, Bacchylides, and other eminent persons. An account of its present state may be found in the Travels of Dr. Clarke, who says, that "it appeared to him to be the best cultivated of any of the Grecian Isles."--Vol. vi. p. 174.

В В В В T.M.






В В В В EVENINGS IN GREECE.




В В В В FIRST EVENING.


В В В В "The sky is bright--the breeze is fair,
В В В В В В В В "And the mainsail flowing, full and free--
В В В В "Our farewell word is woman's prayer,
В В В В В В В В "And the hope before us--Liberty!
В В В В В В В В В В В В "Farewell, farewell.
В В В В В В В В "To Greece we give our shining blades,
В В В В В В В В "And our hearts to you, young Zean Maids!

В В В В "The moon is in the heavens above,
В В В В В В В В "And the wind is on the foaming sea--
В В В В "Thus shines the star of woman's love
В В В В В В В В "On the glorious strife of Liberty!
В В В В В В В В В В В В "Farewell, farewell.
В В В В В В В В "To Greece we give our shining blades,
В В В В В В В В "And our hearts to you, young Zean Maids!"

В В В В В В В В Thus sung they from the bark, that now
В В В В Turned to the sea its gallant prow,
В В В В Bearing within its hearts as brave,
В В В В As e'er sought Freedom o'er the wave;
В В В В And leaving on that islet's shore,
В В В В В В В В Where still the farewell beacons burn,
В В В В Friends that shall many a day look o'er
В В В В В В В В The long, dim sea for their return.

В В В В Virgin of Heaven! speed their way--
В В В В В В В В Oh, speed their way,--the chosen flower,
В В В В Of Zea's youth, the hope and stay
В В В В В В В В Of parents in their wintry hour,
В В В В The love of maidens and the pride
В В В В Of the young, happy, blushing bride,
В В В В Whose nuptial wreath has not yet died--
В В В В All, all are in that precious bark,
В В В В В В В В Which now, alas! no more is seen--
В В В В Tho' every eye still turns to mark
В В В В В В В В The moonlight spot where it had been.

В В В В Vainly you look, ye maidens, sires,
В В В В В В В В And mothers, your beloved are gone!--
В В В В Now may you quench those signal fires,
В В В В В В В В Whose light they long looked back upon
В В В В From their dark deck--watching the flame
В В В В В В В В As fast it faded from their view,
В В В В With thoughts, that, but for manly shame,
В В В В В В В В Had made them droop and weep like you.
В В В В Home to your chambers! home, and pray
В В В В For the bright coming of that day,
В В В В When, blest by heaven, the Cross shall sweep
В В В В The Crescent from the Aegean deep,
В В В В And your brave warriors, hastening back,
В В В В Will bring such glories in their track,
В В В В As shall, for many an age to come,
В В В В Shed light around their name and home.

В В В В В В В В There is a Fount on Zea's isle,
В В В В Round which, in soft luxuriance, smile
В В В В All the sweet flowers, of every kind,
В В В В В В В В On which the sun of Greece looks down,
В В В В В В В В Pleased as a lover on the crown
В В В В His mistress for her brow hath twined,
В В В В When he beholds each floweret there,
В В В В Himself had wisht her most to wear;
В В В В Here bloomed the laurel-rose,[1] whose wreath
В В В В Hangs radiant round the Cypriot shines,
В В В В And here those bramble-flowers, that breathe
В В В В В В В В Their odor into Zante's wines:--
В В В В The splendid woodbine that, as eve,
В В В В В В В В To grace their floral diadems,
В В В В The lovely maids of Patmos weave:--[2]
В В В В And that fair plant whose tangled stems
В В В В Shine like a Nereid's hair,[3] when spread,
В В В В Dishevelled, o'er her azure bed:--
В В В В All these bright children of the clime,
В В В В (Each at its own most genial time,
В В В В The summer, or the year's sweet prime,)
В В В В В В В В Like beautiful earth-stars, adorn
В В В В В В В В The Valley where that Fount is born;
В В В В While round, to grace its cradle green
В В В В Groups of Velani oaks are seen
В В В В Towering on every verdant height--
В В В В Tall, shadowy, in the evening light,
В В В В Like Genii set to watch the birth
В В В В Of some enchanted child of earth--
В В В В Fair oaks that over Zea's vales,
В В В В В В В В Stand with their leafy pride unfurled;
В В В В While Commerce from her thousand sails
В В В В В В В В Scatters their fruit throughout the world![4]

В В В В В В В В 'Twas here--as soon as prayer and sleep
В В В В (Those truest friends to all who weep)
В В В В Had lightened every heart; and made
В В В В Even sorrow wear a softer shade--
В В В В 'Twas here, in this secluded spot,
В В В В В В В В Amid whose breathings calm and sweet
В В В В Grief might be soothed if not forgot,
В В В В В В В В The Zean nymphs resolved to meet
В В В В Each evening now, by the same light
В В В В That saw their farewell tears that night:
В В В В And try if sound of lute and song,
В В В В В В В В If wandering mid the moonlight flowers
В В В В In various talk, could charm along
В В В В В В В В With lighter step, the lingering hours,
В В В В Till tidings of that Bark should come,
В В В В Or Victory waft their warriors home!

В В В В В В В В When first they met--the wonted smile
В В В В Of greeting having gleamed awhile--
В В В В 'Twould touch even Moslem heart to see
В В В В The sadness that came suddenly
В В В В O'er their young brows, when they looked round
В В В В Upon that bright, enchanted ground;
В В В В And thought how many a time with those
В В В В В В В В Who now were gone to the rude wars
В В В В They there had met at evening's close,
В В В В В В В В And danced till morn outshone the stars!

В В В В But seldom long doth hang the eclipse
В В В В В В В В Of sorrow o'er such youthful breasts--
В В В В The breath from her own blushing lips,
В В В В В В В В That on the maiden's mirror rests,
В В В В Not swifter, lighter from the glass,
В В В В Than sadness from her brow doth pass.

В В В В Soon did they now, as round the Well
В В В В В В В В They sat, beneath the rising moon--
В В В В And some with voice of awe would tell
В В В В Of midnight fays and nymphs who dwell
В В В В В В В В In holy founts--while some would time
В В В В Their idle lutes that now had lain
В В В В For days without a single strain;--
В В В В And others, from the rest apart,
В В В В With laugh that told the lightened heart,
В В В В Sat whispering in each other's ear
В В В В Secrets that all in turn would hear;--
В В В В Soon did they find this thoughtless play
В В В В So swiftly steal their griefs away,
В В В В В В В В That many a nymph tho' pleased the while,
В В В В В В В В Reproached her own forgetful smile,
В В В В And sighed to think she could be gay.

В В В В Among these maidens there was one
В В В В В В В В Who to Leucadia[5] late had been--
В В В В Had stood beneath the evening sun
В В В В В В В В On its white towering cliffs and seen
В В В В The very spot where Sappho sung
В В В В Her swan-like music, ere she sprung
В В В В (Still holding, in that fearful leap,
В В В В By her loved lyre,) into the deep,
В В В В And dying quenched the fatal fire,
В В В В At once, of both her heart and lyre.

В В В В В В В В Mutely they listened all--and well
В В В В Did the young travelled maiden tell
В В В В Of the dread height to which that steep
В В В В Beetles above the eddying deep--[6]
В В В В Of the lone sea-birds, wheeling round
В В В В The dizzy edge with mournful sound--
В В В В And of those scented lilies found
В В В В Still blooming on that fearful place--
В В В В As if called up by Love to grace
В В В В The immortal spot o'er which the last
В В В В Bright footsteps of his martyr past!

В В В В В В В В While fresh to every listener's thought
В В В В These legends of Leucadia brought
В В В В All that of Sappho's hapless flame
В В В В Is kept alive, still watcht by Fame--
В В В В The maiden, tuning her soft lute,
В В В В While all the rest stood round her, mute,
В В В В Thus sketched the languishment of soul,
В В В В That o'er the tender Lesbian stole;
В В В В And in a voice whose thrilling tone
В В В В Fancy might deem the Lesbian's own,
В В В В One of those fervid fragments gave,
В В В В В В В В Which still,--like sparkles of Greek Fire,
В В В В Undying, even beneath the wave,--
В В В В В В В В Burn on thro' Time and ne'er expire.


В В В В SONG.


В В В В As o'er her loom the Lesbian Maid
В В В В В В В В In love-sick languor hung her head,
В В В В Unknowing where her fingers strayed,
В В В В В В В В She weeping turned away, and said,
В В В В "Oh, my sweet Mother--'tis in vain--
В В В В В В В В "I cannot weave, as once I wove--
В В В В "So wildered is my heart and brain
В В В В В В В В "With thinking of that youth I love!"

В В В В Again the web she tried to trace,
В В В В В В В В But tears fell o'er each tangled thread;
В В В В While looking in her mother's face,
В В В В В В В В Who watchful o'er her leaned, she said,
В В В В "Oh, my sweet Mother--'tis in vain--
В В В В В В В В "I cannot weave, as once I wove--
В В В В "So wildered is my heart and brain
В В В В В В В В "With thinking of that youth I love!"

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *

В В В В A silence followed this sweet air,
В В В В В В В В As each in tender musing stood,
В В В В Thinking, with lips that moved in prayer,
В В В В В В В В Of Sappho and that fearful flood:
В В В В While some who ne'er till now had known
В В В В В В В В How much their hearts resembled hers,
В В В В Felt as they made her griefs their own,
В В В В В В В В That they too were Love's worshippers.

В В В В В В В В At length a murmur, all but mute,
В В В В So faint it was, came from the lute
В В В В Of a young melancholy maid,
В В В В Whose fingers, all uncertain played
В В В В From chord to chord, as if in chase
В В В В В В В В Of some lost melody, some strain
В В В В Of other times, whose faded trace
В В В В В В В В She sought among those chords again.
В В В В Slowly the half-forgotten theme
В В В В В В В В (Tho' born in feelings ne'er forgot)
В В В В Came to her memory--as a beam
В В В В В В В В Falls broken o'er some shaded spot;--
В В В В And while her lute's sad symphony
В В В В Filled up each sighing pause between;
В В В В And Love himself might weep to see
В В В В В В В В What ruin comes where he hath been--
В В В В As withered still the grass is found
В В В В Where fays have danced their merry round--
В В В В Thus simply to the listening throng
В В В В She breathed her melancholy song:--


В В В В SONG.


В В В В Weeping for thee, my love, thro' the long day,
В В В В Lonely and wearily life wears away.
В В В В Weeping for thee, my love, thro' the long night--
В В В В No rest in darkness, no joy in light!
В В В В Naught left but Memory whose dreary tread
В В В В Sounds thro' this ruined heart, where all lies dead--
В В В В Wakening the echoes of joy long fled!

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *

В В В В В В В В Of many a stanza, this alone
В В В В Had 'scaped oblivion--like the one
В В В В Stray fragment of a wreck which thrown
В В В В With the lost vessel's name ashore
В В В В Tells who they were that live no more.
В В В В В В В В When thus the heart is in a vein
В В В В Of tender thought, the simplest strain
В В В В Can touch it with peculiar power--
В В В В В В В В As when the air is warm, the scent
В В В В Of the most wild and rustic flower
В В В В В В В В Can fill the whole rich element--
В В В В And in such moods the homeliest tone
В В В В That's linked with feelings, once our own--
В В В В With friends or joy gone by--will be
В В В В Worth choirs of loftiest harmony!

В В В В But some there were among the group
В В В В В В В В Of damsels there too light of heart
В В В В To let their spirits longer droop,
В В В В В В В В Even under music's melting art;
В В В В And one upspringing with a bound
В В В В From a low bank of flowers, looked round
В В В В With eyes that tho' so full of light
В В В В В В В В Had still a trembling tear within;
В В В В And, while her fingers in swift flight
В В В В В В В В Flew o'er a fairy mandolin,
В В В В Thus sung the song her lover late
В В В В В В В В Had sung to her--the eve before
В В В В В В В В That joyous night, when as of yore
В В В В All Zea met to celebrate
В В В В В В В В The feast of May on the sea-shore.


В В В В SONG.


В В В В When the Balaika[7]
В В В В В В В В Is heard o'er the sea,
В В В В I'll dance the Romaika
В В В В В В В В By moonlight with thee.
В В В В If waves then advancing
В В В В В В В В Should steal on our play,
В В В В Thy white feet in dancing
В В В В В В В В Shall chase them away.[8]
В В В В When the Balaika
В В В В В В В В Is heard o'er the sea,
В В В В Thou'lt dance the Romaika
В В В В В В В В My own love, with me.

В В В В Then at the closing
В В В В В В В В Of each merry lay,
В В В В How sweet 'tis, reposing
В В В В В В В В Beneath the night ray!
В В В В Or if declining
В В В В В В В В The moon leave the skies,
В В В В We'll talk by the shining
В В В В В В В В Of each other's eyes.

В В В В Oh then how featly
В В В В В В В В The dance we'll renew,
В В В В Treading so fleetly
В В В В В В В В Its light mazes thro':[9]
В В В В Till stars, looking o'er us
В В В В В В В В From heaven's high bowers,
В В В В Would change their bright chorus
В В В В В В В В For one dance of ours!
В В В В When the Balaika
В В В В В В В В Is heard o'er the sea,
В В В В Thou'lt dance the Romaika,
В В В В В В В В My own love, with me.

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *

В В В В How changingly for ever veers
В В В В The heart of youth 'twixt smiles and tears!
В В В В Even as in April the light vane
В В В В Now points to sunshine, now to rain.
В В В В Instant this lively lay dispelled
В В В В В В В В The shadow from each blooming brow,
В В В В And Dancing, joyous Dancing, held
В В В В В В В В Full empire o'er each fancy now.


В В В В But say--what shall the measure be?
В В В В В В В В "Shall we the old Romaika tread,"
В В В В (Some eager asked) "as anciently
В В В В В В В В "'Twas by the maids of Delos led,
В В В В "When slow at first, then circling fast,
В В В В "As the gay spirits rose--at last,
В В В В "With hand in hand like links enlocked,
В В В В В В В В "Thro' the light air they seemed to flit
В В В В "In labyrinthine maze, that mocked
В В В В В В В В "The dazzled eye that followed it?"
В В В В Some called aloud "the Fountain Dance!"--
В В В В В В В В While one young, dark-eyed Amazon,
В В В В Whose step was air-like and whose glance
В В В В В В В В Flashed, like a sabre in the sun,
В В В В Sportively said, "Shame on these soft
В В В В В В В В "And languid strains we hear so oft.
В В В В "Daughters of Freedom! have not we
В В В В В В В В "Learned from our lovers and our sires
В В В В "The Dance of Greece, while Greece was free--
В В В В В В В В "That Dance, where neither flutes nor lyres,
В В В В "But sword and shield clash on the ear
В В В В "A music tyrants quake to hear?
В В В В "Heroines of Zea, arm with me
В В В В "And dance the dance of Victory!"

В В В В Thus saying, she, with playful grace,
В В В В Loosed the wide hat, that o'er her face
В В В В (From Anatolia came the maid)
В В В В В В В В Hung shadowing each sunny charm;
В В В В And with a fair young armorer's aid,
В В В В В В В В Fixing it on her rounded arm,
В В В В A mimic shield with pride displayed;
В В В В Then, springing towards a grove that spread
В В В В В В В В Its canopy of foliage near,
В В В В Plucked off a lance-like twig, and said,
В В В В В В В В "To arms, to arms!" while o'er her head
В В В В В В В В She waved the light branch, as a spear.

В В В В Promptly the laughing maidens all
В В В В Obeyed their Chief's heroic call;--
В В В В Round the shield-arm of each was tied
В В В В В В В В Hat, turban, shawl, as chance might be;
В В В В В В В В The grove, their verdant armory,
В В В В Falchion and lance[10] alike supplied;
В В В В В В В В And as their glossy locks, let free,
В В В В В В В В Fell down their shoulders carelessly,
В В В В You might have dreamed you saw a throng
В В В В В В В В Of youthful Thyads, by the beam
В В В В Of a May moon, bounding along
В В В В В В В В Peneus' silver-eddied stream!

В В В В And now they stept, with measured tread,
В В В В В В В В Martially o'er the shining field;
В В В В Now to the mimic combat led
В В В В (A heroine at each squadron's head),
В В В В В В В В Struck lance to lance and sword to shield:
В В В В While still, thro' every varying feat,
В В В В Their voices heard in contrast sweet
В В В В With some of deep but softened sound
В В В В From lips of aged sires around,
В В В В Who smiling watched their children's play--
В В В В Thus sung the ancient Pyrrhic lay:--


В В В В SONG.


В В В В "Raise the buckler--poise the lance--
В В В В "Now here--now there--retreat--advance!"

В В В В Such were the sounds to which the warrior boy
В В В В В В В В Danced in those happy days when Greece was free;
В В В В When Sparta's youth, even in the hour of joy,
В В В В В В В В Thus trained their steps to war and victory.
В В В В "Raise the buckler--poise the lance--
В В В В "Now here--now there--retreat--advance!"
В В В В Such was the Spartan warriors' dance.
В В В В В В В В "Grasp the falchion--gird the shield--
В В В В "Attack--defend--do all but yield."

В В В В Thus did thy sons, oh Greece, one glorious night,
В В В В В В В В Dance by a moon like this, till o'er the sea
В В В В That morning dawned by whose immortal light
В В В В В В В В They nobly died for thee and liberty![11]
В В В В "Raise the buckler--poise the lance--
В В В В "Now here--now there--retreat--advance!"
В В В В Such was the Spartan heroes' dance.

В В В В Scarce had they closed this martial lay
В В В В When, flinging their light spears away,
В В В В The combatants, in broken ranks.
В В В В В В В В All breathless from the war-field fly;
В В В В And down upon the velvet banks
В В В В В В В В And flowery slopes exhausted lie,
В В В В Like rosy huntresses of Thrace,
В В В В Resting at sunset from the chase.

В В В В "Fond girls!" an aged Zean said--
В В В В One who himself had fought and bled,
В В В В And now with feelings half delight,
В В В В Half sadness, watched their mimic fight--
В В В В "Fond maids! who thus with War can jest--
В В В В "Like Love in Mar's helmet drest,
В В В В "When, in his childish innocence,
В В В В В В В В "Pleased with the shade that helmet flings,
В В В В "He thinks not of the blood that thence
В В В В В В В В "Is dropping o'er his snowy wings.
В В В В "Ay--true it is, young patriot maids,
В В В В В В В В "If Honor's arm still won the fray,
В В В В "If luck but shone on righteous blades,
В В В В В В В В "War were a game for gods to play!
В В В В "But, no, alas!--hear one, who well
В В В В В В В В "Hath tracked the fortunes of the brave--
В В В В "Hear me, in mournful ditty, tell
В В В В В В В В "What glory waits the patriot's grave."


В В В В SONG.


В В В В As by the shore, at break of day,
В В В В A vanquished chief expiring lay.
В В В В Upon the sands, with broken sword,
В В В В В В В В He traced his farewell to the Free;
В В В В And, there, the last unfinished word
В В В В В В В В He dying wrote was "Liberty!"

В В В В At night a Sea-bird shrieked the knell
В В В В Of him who thus for Freedom fell;
В В В В The words he wrote, ere evening came,
В В В В В В В В Were covered by the sounding sea;--
В В В В So pass away the cause and name
В В В В В В В В Of him who dies for Liberty!

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *

В В В В That tribute of subdued applause
В В В В В В В В A charmed but timid audience pays,
В В В В That murmur which a minstrel draws
В В В В В В В В From hearts that feel but fear to praise,
В В В В Followed this song, and left a pause
В В В В Of silence after it, that hung
В В В В Like a fixt spell on every tongue.

В В В В В В В В At length a low and tremulous sound
В В В В Was heard from midst a group that round
В В В В A bashful maiden stood to hide
В В В В Her blushes while the lute she tried--
В В В В Like roses gathering round to veil
В В В В The song of some young nightingale,
В В В В Whose trembling notes steal out between
В В В В The clustered leaves, herself unseen.
В В В В And while that voice in tones that more
В В В В В В В В Thro' feeling than thro' weakness erred,
В В В В Came with a stronger sweetness o'er
В В В В В В В В The attentive ear, this strain was heard:--


В В В В SONG.


В В В В I saw from yonder silent cave,[12]
В В В В В В В В Two Fountains running side by side;
В В В В The one was Memory's limpid wave,
В В В В В В В В The other cold Oblivion's tide.
В В В В "Oh Love!" said I, in thoughtless mood,
В В В В В В В В As deep I drank of Lethe's stream,
В В В В "Be all my sorrows in this flood
В В В В В В В В "Forgotten like a vanisht dream!"

В В В В But who could bear that gloomy blank
В В В В В В В В Where joy was lost as well as pain?
В В В В Quickly of Memory's fount I drank.
В В В В В В В В And brought the past all back again;
В В В В And said, "Oh Love! whate'er my lot,
В В В В В В В В "Still let this soul to thee be true--
В В В В "Rather than have one bliss forgot,
В В В В В В В В "Be all my pains remembered too!"

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В *В В В В В В В В В В В В *В В В В В В В В В В В В *В В В В В В В В В В В В *В В В В В В В В В В В В *

В В В В The group that stood around to shade
В В В В The blushes of that bashful maid,
В В В В Had by degrees as came the lay
В В В В More strongly forth retired away,
В В В В Like a fair shell whose valves divide
В В В В To show the fairer pearl inside:
В В В В For such she was--a creature, bright
В В В В В В В В And delicate as those day-flowers,
В В В В Which while they last make up in light
В В В В В В В В And sweetness what they want in hours.

В В В В В В В В So rich upon the ear had grown
В В В В Her voice's melody--its tone
В В В В Gathering new courage as it found
В В В В An echo in each bosom round--
В В В В That, ere the nymph with downcast eye
В В В В Still on the chords, her lute laid by,
В В В В "Another song," all lips exclaimed,
В В В В And each some matchless favorite named;
В В В В while blushing as her fingers ran
В В В В O'er the sweet chords she thus began:--


В В В В SONG.


В В В В Oh, Memory, how coldly
В В В В В В В В Thou paintest joy gone by:
В В В В Like rainbows, thy pictures
В В В В В В В В But mournfully shine and die.
В В В В Or if some tints thou keepest
В В В В В В В В That former days recall,
В В В В As o'er each line thou weepest,
В В В В В В В В Thy tears efface them all.

В В В В But, Memory, too truly
В В В В В В В В Thou paintest grief that's past;
В В В В Joy's colors are fleeting,
В В В В В В В В But those of Sorrow last.
В В В В And, while thou bringst before us
В В В В В В В В Dark pictures of past ill,
В В В В Life's evening closing o'er us
В В В В В В В В But makes them darker still.

В В В В So went the moonlight hours along,
В В В В In this sweet glade; and so with song
В В В В And witching sounds--not such as they,
В В В В  The cymbalists of Ossa, played,
В В В В To chase the moon's eclipse away,[13]
В В В В В В В В But soft and holy--did each maid
В В В В Lighten her heart's eclipse awhile,
В В В В And win back Sorrow to a smile.

В В В В Not far from this secluded place,
В В В В В В В В On the sea-shore a ruin stood;--
В В В В A relic of the extinguisht race,
В В В В В В В В Who once o'er that foamy flood,
В В В В В В В В When fair Ioulis[14] by the light
В В В В В В В В Of golden sunset on the sight
В В В В В В В В В В В В Of mariners who sailed that sea,
В В В В В В В В Rose like a city of chrysolite
В В В В В В В В В В В В Called from the wave by witchery.
В В В В В В В В This ruin--now by barbarous hands
В В В В В В В В В В В В Debased into a motley shed,
В В В В В В В В Where the once splendid column stands
В В В В В В В В В В В В Inverted on its leafy head--
В В В В В В В В Formed, as they tell in times of old
В В В В В В В В В В В В The dwelling of that bard whose lay
В В В В В В В В Could melt to tears the stern and cold,
В В В В В В В В В В В В And sadden mid their mirth the gay--
В В В В В В В В Simonides,[15] whose fame thro' years
В В В В В В В В В В В В And ages past still bright appears--
В В В В В В В В Like Hesperus, a star of tears!

В В В В В В В В 'Twas hither now--to catch a view
В В В В В В В В В В В В Of the white waters as they played
В В В В В В В В Silently in the light--a few
В В В В В В В В В В В В Of the more restless damsels strayed;
В В В В В В В В And some would linger mid the scent
В В В В В В В В В В В В Of hanging foliage that perfumed
В В В В В В В В The ruined walls; while others went
В В В В В В В В В В В В Culling whatever floweret bloomed

В В В В In the lone leafy space between,
В В В В Where gilded chambers once had been;
В В В В Or, turning sadly to the sea,
В В В В В В В В Sent o'er the wave a sigh unblest
В В В В To some brave champion of the Free--
В В В В Thinking, alas, how cold might be
В В В В В В В В At that still hour his place of rest!

В В В В Meanwhile there came a sound of song
В В В В В В В В From the dark ruins--a faint strain,
В В В В As if some echo that among
В В В В Those minstrel halls had slumbered long
В В В В В В В В Were murmuring into life again.

В В В В But, no--the nymphs knew well the tone--
В В В В В В В В A maiden of their train, who loved
В В В В Like the night-bird to sing alone.
В В В В В В В В Had deep into those ruins roved,
В В В В And there, all other thoughts forgot,
В В В В В В В В Was warbling o'er, in lone delight,
В В В В A lay that, on that very spot,
В В В В В В В В Her lover sung one moonlight night:--


В В В В SONG.


В В В В Ah! where are they, who heard, in former hours,
В В В В The voice of Song in these neglected bowers?
В В В В В В В В They are gone--all gone!

В В В В The youth who told his pain in such sweet tone
В В В В That all who heard him wisht his pain their own--
В В В В В В В В He is gone--he is gone!

В В В В And she who while he sung sat listening by
В В В В And thought to strains like these 'twere sweet to die--
В В В В В В В В She is gone--she too is gone!

В В В В 'Tis thus in future hours some bard will say
В В В В Of her who hears and him who sings this lay--
В В В В В В В В They are gone--they both are gone!

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *

В В В В The moon was now, from heaven's steep,
В В В В В В В В Bending to dip her silvery urn
В В В В Into the bright and silent deep--
В В В В В В В В And the young nymphs, on their return
В В В В From those romantic ruins, found
В В В В Their other playmates ranged around
В В В В The sacred Spring, prepared to tune
В В В В Their parting hymn,[16] ere sunk the moon,
В В В В To that fair Fountain by whose stream
В В В В Their hearts had formed so many a dream.

В В В В В В В В Who has not read the tales that tell
В В В В Of old Eleusis' sacred Well,
В В В В Or heard what legend-songs recount
В В В В Of Syra and its holy Fount,[17]
В В В В Gushing at once from the hard rock
В В В В В В В В Into the laps of living flowers--
В В В В Where village maidens loved to flock,
В В В В В В В В On summer-nights and like the Hours
В В В В Linked in harmonious dance and song,
В В В В Charmed the unconscious night along;
В В В В While holy pilgrims on their way
В В В В В В В В To Delos' isle stood looking on,
В В В В Enchanted with a scene so gay,
В В В В В В В В Nor sought their boats till morning shone.

В В В В Such was the scene this lovely glade
В В В В And its fair inmates now displayed.
В В В В As round the Fount in linked ring
В В В В В В В В They went in cadence slow and light
В В В В And thus to that enchanted Spring
В В В В В В В В Warbled their Farewell for the night:--


В В В В SONG.


В В В В Here, while the moonlight dim
В В В В Falls on that mossy brim,
В В В В Sing we our Fountain Hymn,
В В В В В В В В Maidens of Zea!
В В В В Nothing but Music's strain,
В В В В When Lovers part in pain,
В В В В Soothes till they meet again,
В В В В В В В В Oh, Maids of Zea!

В В В В Bright Fount so clear and cold
В В В В Round which the nymphs of old
В В В В Stood with their locks of gold,
В В В В В В В В Fountain of Zea!
В В В В Not even Castaly,
В В В В Famed tho' its streamlet be,
В В В В Murmurs or shines like thee,
В В В В В В В В Oh, Fount of Zea!

В В В В Thou, while our hymn we sing,
В В В В Thy silver voice shalt bring,
В В В В Answering, answering,
В В В В В В В В Sweet Fount of Zea!
В В В В For of all rills that run
В В В В Sparkling by moon or sun
В В В В Thou art the fairest one,
В В В В В В В В Bright Fount of Zea!

В В В В Now, by those stars that glance
В В В В Over heaven's still expanse
В В В В Weave we our mirthful dance,
В В В В В В В В Daughters of Zea!
В В В В Such as in former days
В В В В Danced they by Dian's rays
В В В В Where the Eurotas strays,
В В В В В В В В Oh, Maids of Zea!

В В В В But when to merry feet
В В В В Hearts with no echo beat,
В В В В Say, can the dance be sweet?
В В В В В В В В Maidens of Zea!
В В В В No, naught but Music's strain,
В В В В When lovers part in pain,
В В В В Soothes till they meet again,
В В В В В В В В Oh, Maids of Zea!




В В В В SECOND EVENING.


В В В В SONG.


В В В В When evening shades are falling
В В В В В В В В O'er Ocean's sunny sleep,
В В В В To pilgrims' hearts recalling
В В В В В В В В Their home beyond the deep;
В В В В When rest o'er all descending
В В В В В В В В The shores with gladness smile,
В В В В And lutes their echoes blending
В В В В В В В В Are heard from isle to isle,
В В В В Then, Mary, Star of the Sea,
В В В В We pray, we pray, to thee!

В В В В The noon-day tempest over,
В В В В В В В В Now Ocean toils no more,
В В В В And wings of halcyons hover
В В В В В В В В Where all was strife before.
В В В В Oh thus may life in closing
В В В В В В В В Its short tempestuous day
В В В В Beneath heaven's smile reposing
В В В В В В В В Shine all its storms away:
В В В В Thus, Mary, Star of the Sea,
В В В В We pray, we pray, to thee!

В В В В On Helle's sea the light grew dim
В В В В As the last sounds of that sweet hymn
В В В В В В В В Floated along its azure tide--
В В В В Floated in light as if the lay
В В В В Had mixt with sunset's fading ray
В В В В В В В В And light and song together died.
В В В В So soft thro' evening's air had breathed
В В В В That choir of youthful voices wreathed
В В В В In many-linked harmony,
В В В В That boats then hurrying o'er the sea
В В В В Paused when they reached this fairy shore,
В В В В And lingered till the strain was o'er.

В В В В Of those young maids who've met to fleet
В В В В In song and dance this evening's hours,
В В В В Far happier now the bosoms beat
В В В В В В В В Than when they last adorned these bowers;
В В В В For tidings of glad sound had come,
В В В В В В В В At break of day from the far isles--
В В В В Tidings like breath of life to some--
В В В В That Zea's sons would soon wing home,
В В В В В В В В Crowded with the light of Victory's smiles
В В В В To meet that brightest of all meeds
В В В В That wait on high, heroic deeds.
В В В В When gentle eyes that scarce for tears
В В В В В В В В Could trace the warrior's parting track,
В В В В Shall like a misty morn that clears
В В В В When the long-absent sun appears
В В В В В В В В Shine out all bliss to hail him back.

В В В В How fickle still the youthful breast!--
В В В В В В В В More fond of change than a young moon,
В В В В No joy so new was e'er possest
В В В В В В В В But Youth would leave for newer soon.
В В В В These Zean nymphs tho' bright the spot
В В В В В В В В Where first they held their evening play
В В В В As ever fell to fairy's lot
В В В В В В В В To wanton o'er by midnight's ray,
В В В В Had now exchanged that sheltered scene
В В В В В В В В For a wide glade beside the sea--
В В В В A lawn whose soft expanse of green
В В В В В В В В Turned to the west sun smilingly
В В В В As tho' in conscious beauty bright
В В В В It joyed to give him light for light.

В В В В And ne'er did evening more serene
В В В В Look down from heaven on lovelier scene.
В В В В Calm lay the flood around while fleet
В В В В В В В В O'er the blue shining element
В В В В Light barks as if with fairy feet

В В В В В В В В The blushing wave, with mainsail free,

В В В В And freshly bright as if just made
В В В В By Love's own hands of new-born light
В В В В Stolen from his mother's star tonight.

В В В В В В В В That mystic curtain backward drew,

В В В В В В В В In half-caught gleams now burst to view.

В В В В With glory lent by heaven alone;

В В В В В В В В Ere yet the simple violet braid,[18]
В В В В Which then adorned her had shone down
В В В В The glory of earth's loftiest crown.

В В В В And all Olympus stood in stone!

В В В В On a soft bank of living green
В В В В Sate a young nymph with her lap full
В В В В В В В В Of the newly gathered flowers, o'er which
В В В В She graceful leaned intent to cull
В В В В В В В В All that was there of hue most rich,
В В В В To form a wreath such as the eye
В В В В Of her young lover who stood by,

В В В В To fix by Painting's rainbow hues.

В В В В The wreath was formed; the maiden raised
В В В В В В В В Her speaking eyes to his, while he--
В В В В Oh not upon the flowers now gazed,
В В В В В В В В But on that bright look's witchery.

В В В В Like light had reached his soul, he caught
В В В В His pencil up and warm and true
В В В В As life itself that love-look drew:

В В В В And forth each kindling feature shone,
В В В В Sweet voices thro' the moonlight air
В В В В В В В В From lips as moonlight fresh and pure
В В В В Thus hailed the bright dream passing there,
В В В В В В В В And sung the Birth of Portraiture.[19]

В В В В В В В В Knew well that by a shady rill

В В В В As generous woman's only is,
В В В В Veiled her own fears to banish his:--
В В В В With frank rebuke but still more vain,
В В В В В В В В Did a rough warrior who stood by
В В В В Call to his mind this martial strain,
В В В В В В В В His favorite once, ere Beauty's eye
В В В В В В В В Had taught his soldier-heart to sigh:--


В В В В SONG.


В В В В March! nor heed those arms that hold thee,
В В В В В В В В Tho' so fondly close they come;
В В В В Closer still will they enfold thee
В В В В В В В В When thou bring'st fresh laurels home.
В В В В Dost thou dote on woman's brow?
В В В В В В В В Dost thou live but in her breath?
В В В В March!--one hour of victory now
В В В В  Wins thee woman's smile till death.

В В В В Oh what bliss when war is over
В В В В В В В В Beauty's long-missed smile to meet.
В В В В And when wreaths our temples cover
В В В В В В В В Lay them shining at her feet.
В В В В Who would not that hour to reach
В В В В В В В В Breathe out life's expiring sigh,--
В В В В Proud as waves that on the beach
В В В В В В В В Lay their war-crests down and die.

В В В В There! I see thy soul is burning--
В В В В В В В В She herself who clasps thee so
В В В В Paints, even now, thy glad returning,
В В В В В В В В And while clasping bids thee go.
В В В В One deep sigh to passion given,
В В В В В В В В One last glowing tear and then--
В В В В March!--nor rest thy sword till Heaven
В В В В В В В В Brings thee to those arms again.

В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *В В В В В В В В В В В В  *

В В В В Even then ere loath their hands could part
В В В В В В В В A promise the youth gave which bore
В В В В Some balm unto the maiden's heart,
В В В В В В В В That, soon as the fierce fight was o'er,
В В В В To home he'd speed, if safe and free--
В В В В В В В В Nay, even if dying, still would come,
В В В В So the blest word of "Victory!"
В В В В В В В В Might be the last he'd breathe at home.
В В В В "By day," he cried, "thou'lt know my bark;
В В В В "But should I come thro' midnight dark,
В В В В "A blue light on the prow shall tell
В В В В "That Greece hath won and all is well!"

В В В В Fondly the maiden every night,
В В В В Had stolen to seek that promised light;
В В В В Nor long her eyes had now been turned
В В В В From watching when the signal burned.
В В В В Signal of joy--for her, for all--
В В В В В В В В Fleetly the boat now nears the land,
В В В В While voices from the shore-edge call
В В В В В В В В For tidings of the long-wished band.

В В В В Oh the blest hour when those who've been
В В В В В В В В Thro' peril's paths by land or sea
В В В В Locked in our arms again are seen
В В В В В В В В Smiling in glad security;
В В В В When heart to heart we fondly strain,
В В В В В В В В Questioning quickly o'er and o'er--
В В В В Then hold them off to gaze affain
В В В В В В В В And ask, tho' answered oft before,
В В В В В В В В If they indeed are ours once more?

В В В В Such is the scene so full of joy
В В В В Which welcomes now this warrior-boy,
В В В В As fathers, sisters, friends all run
В В В В Bounding to meet him--all but one
В В В В Who, slowest on his neck to fall,
В В В В Is yet the happiest of them all.

В В В В And now behold him circled round
В В В В В В В В With beaming faces at that board,
В В В В While cups with laurel foliage crowned,
В В В В В В В В Are to the coming warriors poured--
В В В В Coming, as he, their herald, told,
В В В В With blades from victory scarce yet cold,
В В В В With hearts untouched by Moslem steel
В В В В And wounds that home's sweet breath will heal.

В В В В "Ere morn," said he,--and while he spoke
В В В В В В В В Turned to the east, where clear and pale
В В В В The star of dawn already broke--
В В В В В В В В "We'll greet on yonder wave their sail!"
В В В В Then wherefore part? all, all agree
В В В В В В В В To wait them here beneath this bower;
В В В В And thus, while even amidst their glee,
В В В В Each eye is turned to watch the sea,
В В В В В В В В With song they cheer the anxious hour.


В В В В SONG.


В В В В "'Tis the Vine! 'tis the Vine!" said the cup-loving boy
В В В В As he saw it spring bright from the earth,
В В В В And called the young Genii of Wit, Love, and Joy,
В В В В В В В В To witness and hallow its birth.
В В В В The fruit was full grown, like a ruby it flamed
В В В В В В В В Till the sunbeam that kist it looked pale;
В В В В "'Tis the Vine! 'tis the Vine!" every Spirit exclaimed
В В В В В В В В "Hail, hail to the Wine-tree, all hail!"

В В В В First, fleet as a bird to the summons Wit flew,
В В В В В В В В While a light on the vine-leaves there broke
В В В В In flashes so quick and so brilliant all knew
В В В В В В В В T'was the light from his lips as he spoke.
В В В В "Bright tree! let thy nectar but cheer me," he cried,
В В В В В В В В "And the fount of Wit never can fail:"
В В В В "'Tis the Vine! 'tis the Vine!" hills and valleys reply,
В В В В В В В В "Hail, hail to the Wine-tree, all hail!"

В В В В Next Love as he leaned o'er the plant to admire
В В В В В В В В Each tendril and cluster it wore,
В В В В From his rosy mouth sent such a breath of desire,
В В В В В В В В As made the tree tremble all o'er.
В В В В Oh! never did flower of the earth, sea, or sky,
В В В В В В В В Such a soul-giving odor inhale:
В В В В "'Tis the Vine! 'tis the Vine!" all re-echo the cry,
В В В В В В В В "Hail, hail to the Wine-tree, all hail!"

В В В В Last, Joy, without whom even Love and Wit die,
В В В В В В В В Came to crown the bright hour with his ray;
В В В В And scarce had that mirth-waking tree met his eye,
В В В В В В В В When a laugh spoke what Joy could not say;--
В В В В A laugh of the heart which was echoed around
В В В В В В В В Till like music it swelled on the gale:
В В В В "T is the Vine! 'tis the Vine!" laughing myriads resound,
В В В В "Hail, hail to the Wine-tree, all hail!"
